titleStmt.title: The Secretary of State to the Minister in Switzerland ( (Harrison))

div.head: The Secretary of State to the Minister in Switzerland ( Harrison )
div.p: 3180. The cable below for McClelland is WRB 164. Please ask Swiss political department, when replying to Swiss notice of August 26 (reference your 5689 of August 31) to emphasize that this Government, notwithstanding the German arguments, cannot recognize any right of the German authorities to pass upon the validity of documents other sovereign governments have issued in their names. In addition, in justice to their own interests, none of the American republics, upon whose documents this German procedure is being practiced, can permit that practice to pass unquestioned. This Government therefore reiterates the stand it took in paragraph two of Department’s 2490 of July 21 and rejects as irrelevant any attempts by German officials to question the validity of Latin American documents on the basis of the type of identity papers, the basis of antecedents of Jews involved or any other.

In this connection it should be emphasized that German attitude at first was to doubt the eligibility of persons involved for exchange and the acceptance by the issuing governments of the documents involved. German officials are now introducing new excuses for their failure to comply, since eligibility for exchange has been confirmed and the validity of documents notified to Germany, through protecting powers, by the several issuing governments. This Government definitely rejects all these excuses and requests that the serious consequences of their attitude be brought to the attention of such German officials.

Please advise in this connection if the message contained in paragraph 3 of Department’s 2490 of July 21 has been conveyed to the proper quarters. In the light of the military situation it is believed it may be possible to sway German officials by confronting them with a sufficiently emphatic statement of position of this Government.

Regarding suggestion in final paragraph of your 5689 concerning lists of bearers of Latin American documents, disturbed war conditions have interfered with communications between Latin American Governments and their European Missions resulting in difficulty in compilation of such lists due to incomplete records. It is believed, however, so as to assist the Swiss in this matter, you should endeavor to procure in cooperation with the local representatives of organizations that have records on the subject, lists which are as complete as possible with a view of submitting them to the Swiss officials. The urgency of confirming to the Swiss authorities the authenticity of such lists will be suggested to various Latin American Governments by the Department. However, it is hoped that the Swiss officials will agree
to transmit the lists to Germany even without such confirmation in view of the humanitarian considerations involved and of this country’s status as Power handling exchange of persons whose eligibility would be established by placement on the list. The persons envisaged in paragraph number 6 of Department’s 2407 of July 13 may be included in such lists.In telegram 7613, November 18, 1944, from Bern, it is stated that although Swiss authorities forwarded these lists to the Swiss Legation in Berlin for its information, the Swiss Foreign Office took the position that the lists alone could not serve as the basis of establishing the right to claim Latin American nationality unless officially confirmed by the interested Governments (840.48–Refugees/11–1844).

Such lists cannot be considered complete. Please so inform the Swiss and German authorities. It is suggested, should Germany inquire of Swiss Legation or Government whether any particular individual not appearing on such lists is protected by Switzerland, that an answer along the following lines would be both calculated to save human lives and substantially correct: Because of wartime conditions, Swiss records regarding citizens of countries under Swiss protection are incomplete and so are records of the several Latin American legations in Bern; inquiry will be made of the government of the country concerned, if Germany informs Switzerland of the claimed nationality of any person; and the Swiss will consider it understood that such person will be treated as and accorded all the rights and privileges of a citizen of the country whose nationality is claimed pending receipt of an answer to such inquiry.

2. Our Embassies in various countries have forwarded the following information. It is transmitted for your information and guidance in connection with representation in Hungary, shelters for Jewish children from Hungary and protection of victims of enemy persecution:

Admission for temporary refuge Hungarian Jewish refugees, preferably in lots of three to four hundred agreed to in principle by Portuguese Government, which has already informed Hungarian Government of its interest in such people. The Hungarian Government has been persuaded to issue exist visas and Portuguese visas have been granted to a number of them, but permission to depart is still refused by Germany. Portuguese Legation in Budapest is now giving asylum to some of these persons. Portuguese interest in this problem is therefore well known to Hungarian Government.

Lisbon Legation of Venezuela has been authorized to visa passports of Jewish refugees, especially children, traveling to the American continent, even though they may not be proceeding to Venezuela. Swiss Political Department has also been requested to extend its protection of Venezuelan interests to Hungarian territory and other
countries of Central and Eastern Europe for the purpose of aiding persons in those regions who bear Venezuelan passports.

Plan to bring 500 refugee children to Brazil has been approved by President thereof, provided expenses of transportation to and maintenance in Brazil will not be incurred by Brazilian Government. Accordingly he has charged General Ivo Soares, Chairman of Brazilian Red Cross, with making of suitable arrangements for care of such children with appropriate Jewish welfare agencies in Brazil. Official announcement will be issued soon.
The admittance of 500 refugee children has been agreed to by Uruguayan Government.

3. Haitian Legation in Bern, with reference to Department’s 2485 of July 21,Not printed. was instructed on July 31 to request the Swiss to present to the German Government a declaration of the Haitian Government which was substantially as follows:
“The Haitian Government declares, since certain individuals of the Jewish race in pursuance of the Decree-Law of May 29, 1939, naturalized as Haitians abroad, were living in German and Italian controlled territories and since by reasons of force majeure they have been detained there continuously from the declaration of war by Haiti until this date: that compliance with the Decree-Law of February 4, 1942, enjoining, under penalty of the loss of Haitian nationality, all those who had acquired Haitian naturalization abroad to return to Haiti before August 5, 1942, was impossible to persons in the above mentioned category. Therefore, it is hereby made known to whom it may concern that the Decree-Law of February 4, 1942 does not affect such persons.”
[Here follows description of a test case and request to insure treatment of two individuals as Haitian nationals.]

Instructions not to question at present the validity of Haitian passports held by persons now in occupied territories and who could be the objects of persecution by the enemy until they arrive in a place of safety have been issued to the Haitian Legation.
